ai_strategy.summary: University of Georgia's public AI posture combines published guidance, teaching resources, research centers, AI-relevant programs, and research computing. Public evidence points to distributed governance rather than a verified standalone chief AI officer.

key_provisions: Use appropriate or approved AI tools within institutional data rules
Faculty should set clear course-level AI expectations
Users remain responsible for accuracy, attribution, privacy, and academic integrity
Sensitive data should not be entered into unapproved public tools
ai_policy.summary: UGA publishes EITS and teaching guidance for AI use, emphasizing data stewardship, acceptable use, and course-level faculty decisions.

bodies.name: AI technology and teaching guidance owners
bodies.scope: Distributed responsibility for AI tool guidance, teaching support, and research coordination.

governance_structure.summary: University of Georgia's AI governance is distributed across IT, teaching support, and research units.

initiatives.title: University of Georgia AI guidance and acceptable-use resources
initiatives.description: UGA publishes EITS and teaching guidance for AI use, emphasizing data stewardship, acceptable use, and course-level faculty decisions.
initiatives.significance: Institution-level guidance is the baseline signal for responsible AI adoption.

investment.description: Policy and guidance work; no dollar amount disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: AI use is permitted within privacy, security, course, and attribution guardrails.

initiatives.title: University of Georgia generative AI teaching resources
initiatives.description: University of Georgia publishes faculty-facing resources for syllabus language, assignments, assessment, and student expectations around generative AI.
initiatives.significance: Teaching guidance moves AI adoption from conduct enforcement into course design.

investment.description: Faculty support resources; no amount disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: Faculty set course-level rules and students need clear disclosure and permitted-use expectations.

initiatives.description: University of Georgia has AI, machine learning, data science, policy, health, or autonomy research structures that organize faculty and graduate research.
initiatives.significance: Dedicated research structures are a core marker of R1 AI maturity.

investment.description: Research center/institute support; aggregate AI-specific amount not disclosed.

initiatives.title: Artificial Intelligence graduate programs
initiatives.description: University of Georgia offers AI-relevant degrees, certificates, bootcamps, or structured learning pathways in AI, machine learning, data science, analytics, or computing.
initiatives.significance: AI credentials connect R1 research capacity to student and employer demand.

initiatives.title: University of Georgia research computing for AI workloads
initiatives.description: The Georgia Advanced Computing Resource Center provides research computing resources for UGA, including AI and data-intensive research workloads.
initiatives.significance: AI maturity depends on scalable compute as well as policy and curricula.

investment.description: Research computing infrastructure; AI-specific public amount not disclosed.

initiatives.title: University of Georgia responsible and equitable AI access
initiatives.description: University of Georgia's public AI resources stress transparent expectations, responsible use, and access-aware teaching or public-interest AI work.
initiatives.significance: Equity evidence distinguishes mature AI adoption from simple tool availability.

investment.description: Access and responsible-use work; no amount disclosed.

initiatives.policy_implications: Clear AI expectations reduce unequal access, ambiguous enforcement, and privacy risk.

initiatives.title: UGA GenAI teaching tools and AI Toolbox
initiatives.description: UGA CTL and AI Hub resources identify free UGA-licensed tools for students, faculty, and staff and provide teaching guidance around opportunities, concerns, assignments, and appropriate use.
initiatives.significance: UGA pairs campuswide protected tools with teaching-center support for AI-aware pedagogy and assessment.

computing_infrastructure.supercomputer_name: Sapelo2 / Georgia Advanced Computing Resource Center
computing_infrastructure.summary: UGA's GACRC Sapelo2 GPU documentation lists open-access GPU resources including 12 nodes with four H100 GPUs, 14 nodes with four A100 GPUs, and 12 nodes with four L4 GPUs, totaling 152 disclosed regular-partition GPUs before any buy-in-only GPUs.

equity_and_access_initiatives.title: Responsible and transparent AI use
equity_and_access_initiatives.description: Public guidance emphasizes clarity, responsible use, and access-aware teaching or research practices.

regulatory_context.summary: University of Georgia operates under federal education, privacy, and research obligations plus University System of Georgia system rules. This sweep found institutional AI guidance but no separate campus-specific AI statute.

narrative_summary: University of Georgia is an R1 institution whose public AI posture is visible in responsible-use guidance, classroom resources, AI-relevant research structures, credentials or workforce programming, and research computing. The campus permits AI experimentation with guardrails rather than presenting a simple prohibition. Faculty-facing materials make course policy and assessment design central to implementation, while technology guidance emphasizes privacy, security, attribution, and responsible use. Research evidence comes from AI, machine-learning, data-science, policy, health, autonomy, or computing organizations, supported by high-performance or research computing resources. The profile is strongest on public policy resources, research centers, programs, and infrastructure, and weaker on exact budgets, enterprise license counts, adoption metrics, and named executive ownership. Overall, the institution appears to be in a growing-to-operational phase: AI work is real and distributed across functions, but public evidence does not yet show a single centralized AI operating model with disclosed outcomes.

No institution-specific AI litigation or major controversy was confirmed.
